narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 27, Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st (well below the typical 5th or 6th decile), Year 1 decile of 2nd (also lower than average), upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and predicted a 52.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which stands out as unusually low compared to peers and signaled to the model a significant dip in performance relative to others at your school. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's reasonably close to the average of 3.21, showing solid prior academic ability. Your family's upper-middle income also helped somewhat, nudging the model slightly toward expecting a pass, likely reflecting access to resources that support many successful students. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT score of 27 is notably below the average of around 37, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your full-time status aligns with the vast majority who pass. While these stronger negative signals from later-year grades outweighed the positives, it's a close call at 52.9%, meaning small improvements could shift outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the mid-30s range, strengthening your later undergraduate or graduate grades to improve decile rankings, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Many students in similar positions reapply successfully after targeted prep, like bar-focused tutoring or additional coursework. You're clearly capable, given your GPA's positive pull, so focusing on these areas can demonstrate growth to future reviewers. Keep pushing—law schools value persistence in applicants like you.
